steps.prompt: Which word is an interrogative pronoun?

hints.qui: 'Qui' is a relative pronoun here, introducing the relative clause
hints.ventis: 'Ventis' is a noun meaning 'winds' in the dative/ablative

steps.prompt: What case is the relative pronoun 'qui'?
steps.reveal: qui = who (nom. masc. sg., subject of 'imperat')

hints.ablative: Ablative masculine would be 'quo' — 'qui' here is nominative, subject of 'imperat'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'cui' — 'qui' is nominative subject of the relative clause
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'quem' — 'qui' is nominative here

hints.to hinder: That would be 'impedire' — 'imperat' comes from 'imperare' (to command)
hints.to prepare: That would be 'parare' — 'imperat' comes from 'imperare' (to command)
hints.to conquer: That would be 'vincere' — 'imperat' comes from 'imperare' (to command)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'imperet' — 'imperat' with -a- is indicative (1st conj.)
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'imperavit' — 'imperat' is present indicative
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'imperabat' — 'imperat' is present indicative

hints.impedio, impedire, impedivi, impeditum: These are the parts of 'impedire' (to hinder)
hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are the parts of 'parare' (to prepare)
hints.aperio, aperire, aperui, apertum: These are the parts of 'aperire' (to open)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'venti' — 'ventis' is dative/ablative plural
hints.ablative: While 'ventis' could be ablative, 'imperare' takes a dative object
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'ventorum' — 'ventis' is dative with 'imperare'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'venti' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'venti' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'venti' ends in -i

steps.prompt: The verb 'imperare' takes what kind of object?

hints.accusative: Most verbs take accusative objects, but 'imperare' governs the dative case
hints.genitive: Some verbs take genitive objects, but 'imperare' governs the dative
hints.ablative: Some verbs take ablative objects, but 'imperare' governs the dative

hints.accusative: Accusative masculine of 'hic' would be 'hunc' — 'hic' is nominative
hints.dative: Dative of 'hic' would be 'huic' — 'hic' is nominative
hints.ablative: Ablative masculine of 'hic' would be 'hoc' — 'hic' is nominative

translation: Who is this who commands the winds?
